title: 2025/03/06 - Petitions
displayTitle: 2025-03-06 - Petitions Committee - Petition of Ann Chapman: Amend the HART Act to allow more flexible timeframes for embryo/gamete storage
topic: Petition of Ann Chapman: Amend the HART Act to allow more flexible timeframes for embryo/gamete storage
description: Petition of Ann Chapman: Amend the HART Act to allow more flexible timeframes for embryo/gamete storage Hearing of Evidence: Ann Chapman ACART: Debra Wilson, Chair Fertility Associates: Suzanne Sherwin, General Manager Ministry of Health: Saskia Patton, Manager, Ethics Ministry of Justice: Helen McDonald, Manager, Policy Group Jennie Marjoribanks, Principal Advisor, Policy Group RANZCOG: Prof Cindy Farquhar, Dean of Research & Policy
descriptionSummary: Petition of Ann Chapman: Amend the HART Act to allow more flexible timeframes for embryo/gamete storage. Ann Chapman. ACART

chunks.text: Good afternoon everyone. Good afternoon. Hello. Hello. Well, welcome. Unfortunately, we're nicely on time today because we are, um, we're always dependent on when question type finishes, so let's thank you for your patience anyway, right now.

Um, you can take, now what we'll do is just, and for the sake of those other submitters in the room, we'll just, uh, introduce ourselves. I'm Greg O'Connor, the chairman of the, um, committee. I'm Greg Fleming, the deputy chair and the MP for Mongo, Kiki, et Cetera. I'm committee permanent member Paul Garcia, uh, national MP for newly And Chapman.

And online we have, uh, Francisco Hernandez, um, who will be joining us, questions if necessary. Yeah. Oh, he's there. F Francisco Hernandez. I'm, uh, one of the Green mps and I'm subbing in for Ka Carter, who is the permanent member of this committee. Alright, now we've got you for 10 minutes.

Um, now, Ms. Chapman? Yes. Ms. Chapman? Yes. You can call me Anna Anne. Okay, thank you Ann. Um, and we have you and I have some friends who are here supporting me. Very good. Well, you're welcome. In Case I get too nervous, If you like. And we'll, so Tim, take your submissions as read, um, but speak to the highlights and hopefully leave time for questions. Welcome.

Thank you for the opportunity to speak. And my petition follows many letters, articles, and submissions that I've written on the subject of Section 10 of the Heart Act, which have been unsuccessful. Amending Section 10 is simply not a priority for the government. Uh, and I understand that and making submissions.

And finally, this petitions, I make no judgment on any person or organization, which has been part of the process. Fertility Associates have done, um, all they could to assist me and I thank them for that. While my petition relates to the experience of my grandson who got leukemia when he was 16, it is not about him and not receiving the first advisory letter from Fertility Associates advising that his 10 year storage was up, he suddenly found himself in the position that he was in danger of never being able to have children

of his own at a time of his own. Interesting. His application for an extension was declined, which added to the stress. And it made me also wonder why the power to decline or accept, uh, applications for extensions rest in the hands of anonymous body rather than politic politicians that a group of people should have the power to decide whether or not a young person should have children is to me and the concept of natural justice, it's too late for my grandson.

So my position is for those families who do not have the financial resources to employ a lawyer to pay for a courier and for storage in Australia, which is what we had to do, that is what my family had to do. And it was at some cost to us and it was to preserve our grandson's.

Right. To have a child at his time of his own choosing. So my petition is for those families who do not have an old trooper like me able to write to advocate and who has some knowledge of the system of governance. So my petition is essentially about natural justice and human rights.

Simply put Section 10 of the Heart Act does not meet the needs of young people today who have had cancer. Alright, well look, thank you very much. Could, could I just ask a question? He was notified or, or who he was notified that the, um, time was up too late.

Is that correct? Well, He moved house in the first letter advising him that he needed to apply for an extension to be able for fertility associates to be able to continue storing his semen. Didn't get to him. Alright, so the second letter he did receive and that told him your time's up. Alright.

Did the first letter, obviously that was an advisory, It was an advisory letter. As I understand it, we still have not seen it. Well, if that were to be fixed, that the advisory letter had to be, um, what used to be the old registered mail.

Yeah. Um, I'm not sure what the modern equivalent is, but the onus was on the, uh, the, the organization holding the sample to ensure that the notification was delivered. That would be helpful. Would That, would that be helpful As well? That would be helpful, but I think, um, more and more people, more and more young people are getting cancer and, you know, it worries me that, that they don't have the mental, physical financial resources to do, to do what I've done.

And if I can just ask one other question, do you think there should be any time limit it? No, I don't think there should be a time limit it. Well, I can see the logic in having a time limit if you store your sea, if you get sick and you need to store your semen when you were in your thirties. Yes. I mean, there is some logic in having a timeline, but then, but 16 and 26, you're barely starting a life.

You're barely getting into the business of having a long-term relationship. Kids these days are having children later. And And from your knowledge of the system, would he likely to have been allowed to continue to store the sample? Had he, Had he asked for, For had He got the first letter? Yes.

Uh, your understanding is that the criteria that exists? He would've I been, I I think that's, I think that's right. You can apply for an extension and it is granted. And how long is that extension for? We may be able to get That. That's right. Don't, I'm sorry. So, so we, we may be able to get, we have some other submitters who'll be able to fill in that. Yeah. So look, I'm sorry that don, these, these, as I read your submission, these are the ones that came to me.

Yeah. As the chair, I suppose I've got the, the, um, the privilege. You've been able to ask those questions. But, um, other members, do you have questions? I, I do. Yes. Yeah. Hello. Um, thank you so much for both your written and your oral submission today. Thank you for taking this cause up. I'm really looking forward to, um, digging into the detail of why the legislation operates where it is.

So echoing all of, uh, the cheers questions, just, um, two for me. One, just, um, picking up on that question around should there be a time limit, would you suggest that maybe they seem be destroyed when the person dies themselves? Absolutely. Right. Yes. But that, so that, that would be your recommendation at that point?

Yes. I hadn't even thought about that. Okay. I, Yeah, I, I mean, I'm a grandmother. I'm nearly 80 and I didn't, don't think of my grandchild dying. So that never came into my, um, my thought process at all. Yeah. But I think there was some logic in destroying, um, samples, any samples unless that person specifically asked for it to be continue to be stowed. Stowed. Okay, cool.

One of the arguments that, um, counter arguments may be that were, that, um, sample then to be used down the track that there could be issues of liability, financial liability for the individual. So there's, it does open up, but, but I guess your answer would be, well they, they're gonna be there at any stage, um, anyway, so any perhaps comment on that?

No, I don't have any thoughts about that. I'm sorry, Members, any further questions? I just, And sorry if this wasn't clear. So, so you, because of the resources of your family, you were able to, to store his semen in Australia? Is that, what is that In Australia. Okay. So We had to get, um, yeah, a courier. Okay.

And, and the cost of the fair and five years storage in Australia, and then the cost of the fare of the courier back. So it is a substantial Amount. Okay. So it's back in New Zealand now? No, it's in Australia. It's in Australia for five years, at which point you need to renew. And so you are, what you are hoping is that our legislation, our regime has changed by that stage that when it comes back then I was hoping by then I might have a great grandchild.

What's, um, from your knowledge, and again, we might be able to get some assistance data on in Australia, so it's a five year threshold. What's gonna happen after five years? There was a five year, um, choice that my son made to pay for storage. Right. I don't know if there is a threshold there or not. Well, that, that's something we may be able to, from the nods, we may be able to get that knowledge from somebody else.

Submitters. Well, look, I think it's quite clear, um, things it submission saying for your work. And, uh, this is one thing about our petitions committee, you know, we get, um, matters come before us that, uh, there is nowhere else to take them. And as a result, I like to think we've become very well informed about some of these issues. So thank you.

So can I ask what happens now? Well, we are going to hear now from, um, organizations, associated organizations, um, with, and then, then we will then repair, prepare a report, the recommendation, um, back to parliament and you'll get a copy of that report.

That'll be a public available report. So our job really now is to hear from Every everybody else From as many people as we can to improve the quality of, of our recommendations. Yep. And So can you recommend, um, a change the legislation? Mm. Is that part of We can't change it. No, No. I know. Can't change it. But can you recommend a change?

We can recommend the government then now instigate, um, the, the the process. The process, the relevant ministry. Yeah. And minister then can, um, undertake to do this, um, to do, to do work on this. We, we are not, um, we like to think we're quite powerful as a petition committee, but we can't actually unfortunately instigate the, the, um, the uh, um, legislation directly from here.
But good question. Very good question. Thank you very much. Thank you.

Every, every, every grandson should have a, uh, grand grandmother right now. Uh, we're gonna hear from advisory committee on assisted reproduction technology, and we're getting, they're coming in via Zoom. I understand Deborah Wilson. We have, Can you hear me?

We, we have, we hear you. And Uh, there we go. We can, Uh, thank you Deborah. Uh, Good afternoon everyone. Um, so my name is Debra Wilson. I'm the new chair of the Advisory Comm Committee on Assisted Reproduction, um, taking over the role in, um, end of December of last year. Prior to that, I was a member of a, a for, for three years as the legal expert.

Um, I'm also a professor of medical law at the University of Canterbury. Alright, look. Well, thank you. So, um, did you hear, you've heard the previous submitters? Yes, I, um, yes. The previous submitter I heard, yes. Oh, Good. So, um, now we have, we've read your submission, but we're looking forward to hearing from you and 10 minutes and hopefully leave time for questions. Oh, I'll try my best. Um, I just wanna start by acknowledging the young man and the family in this situation, um, to go through the cancer treatment and survive it

and then to be hit with this kind of situation. Um, I don't have the words. It's, it's, it's horrible. Um, when this came before a a TI just remember the feeling in the room being that we really wanted to help if there was any way that we possibly could. And we saw the, the sort of unfairness of the situation and how what was happening was sort of contrary to the PR purposes and principles of the HEART Act.

And we really wanted to find a way to tell ECAT that they could hear this application and, and see where it goes. Um, so we spent a lot of time and effort and a lot of nights trying to brainstorm to see how this application could be heard by Kart. But we ended up realizing that actually we, we simply couldn't, um, that because the, the tenure year limit is so fixed in the Heart Act, AART doesn't have jurisdiction to give kart any advice or guidance, and therefore Kart just simply could not get the application.

And we, we couldn't find a way around that. Um, so the, the only thing that, that Aart could actually do is perform it other function, which is to provide advice to the Minister of Health as to possible changes, um, to the HEART Act. And so that's what we did. Um, in May of last year, we, we provided advice, um, suggesting an amendment to the act, which would allow for late applications to be heard by kart in, in certain situations.

And we gave some examples of when that, that might be appropriate. And we told the minister that we would, um, follow this quite quick advice up with some more, um, detailed supplementary advice when we had the time to really debate and, and see where that could go. Um, this supplementary advice is just in its final drafting process at the moment, and we intend that to go to the Minister, um, within the next week or so.

Um, and the other thing that, that we've been doing is in September of last year, um, ACOT and ECOT presented a joint submission on the improving arrangements for Surrogacy bill. And we realized that that Bill had a couple of, um, minor changes to the Heart Act snuck in that weren't quite surrogacy related.

And so we thought we would take the opportunity and our submission on that, um, to, to repeat, um, that maybe when the surrogacy bill is going forward, um, we could also sneak in some amendments to Section 10 of the Heart Act because egg, egg and embryo freezing is a little bit related to surrogacy. Um, so we are aware of the issue and we are looking for ways that, that we can address it in the most appropriate way.

Um, so that's kind of an overview. I'm happy to answer any questions that you have about our work. Alright, members? No, that's very answer. Yep. Um, Mr. Chair? Yes. Yes. Um, Deborah, what is the science base, um, of the length of time, uh, why is it prescribed at, at tap At the 10 year mark?

Okay, this is, now I'm talking as, as an academic who's done research into this. Um, if you trace it back, the 10 years came in in our 2004 act, right. Which we borrowed from the UK Human Fertilization and Embryology Act. And they got that from the 1984 Warnock report. Right? So it was a very long time ago, 40 years ago that they picked the 10 year period.

Um, several reasons. First was the, in 1984, they weren't sure about the science, so they didn't know how long you could free sperm and then defrost it and have it work. So they said, well, 10 years, and then we'll see how the science develops. The science has developed very well since then and now we're, we are looking at 50 or 60 years and the, the sperm or eggs are still very viable.

Um, another reason was that they wanted to ensure, um, that people just check in that you don't store material and leave it there forever because things change. Um, they wanted to avoid the situation where maybe relationship breakups might happen, and then later on someone wants to, to use it and there's a conflict.

One party wants, wants to use, the other party does not. So having a renewal process every 10 years would make sure that no events like that have happened and would also, um, require people to just rethink their plans every 10 years. Um, there was a concern that you don't want families with kids that are 20, 30 years difference in age.

So it was really just a way of making sure there was a check-in policy every 10 years. Um, and also certainty for clinics. Um, while sperm is obviously not very big in itself, the storage containers are actually quite big and they cost money. Um, and so there, there was a concern that if people just stored and left them there forever, um, the people would, um, they would run outta space.

So those were the reasons, um, they, they, the scientific basis is, is not relevant anymore. Um, the need to keep checking in and making sure that people still want to keep them, um, is still valid. But other countries like the United Kingdom have now addressed that in different ways than requiring a fresh application every 10 years.

Right. Thank you Deborah. And, and your view is that, you know, you gave a good exhaustive list there of rationale. Do you believe that any or all of those rationale are still pertinent? Um, I think it's important that you don't just freeze forever.

That there is some point where, um, if I've got my eggs frozen, that I sit down and say, do I still want them frozen? Do I intend to use them? I think there's a, there's validity in that because they are potential life and there should be a level of, of respect there. Um, but I'm not sure it's necessary to go through an application process.

So the United Kingdom, which we borrowed their original model of 10 years, and then you can reapply, um, in the last couple of years they've gone to, um, almost open-ended. You can store for up to 55 years, but every 10 years you just have to re-consent. Oh, okay. So that, that does the, the thinking about it getting in touch with the fertility clinic, but having it as a consent rather than an application kind of addresses the situation where if a late application occurs and you're, you're in trouble.

Right. The consent can can happen a bit later and it would still be okay. So I do think it's important that we keep checking in, but not linked to an application. Alright. And in your experience, a case like, um, Mr. Chapman's, um, grandson, it sounds like it'd be a no brainer to grant an extension in that situation.

Am I, it would no brainer. It's not a very good medical technical term. Yeah. But, um, it would probably fulfill the criteria, um, quite easily. I think it would've fulfilled the criteria. Yeah. Alright. And that, it sounds like you're accepting that it's not a bad idea to have some sort of a threshold, even if it's as little as a check in and advising, asking people that, is this still relevant? Do you, Do you think? I think so. I think it's always a good idea. Yeah.

And just, can I just ask, and this is not something, what's the cost of storage is always born by the donor, is that right by the family or the, Um, in the full cost in this particular case. So the case of a minor who's storing, because they're going through chemotherapy that is government funded. So the first 10 years will often be funded by the government.

And that's also, um, why I think this case kind of got into a little bit of the issue that it had. Um, if someone is storing because they're going through IVF treatment or something at the moment, then they pay themselves and every year they get a bill, which is kind of a reminder. You've gotta pay, and by the way, you've only got so many years left, but for a case with a minor where it's sort of government funded for the first 10 years, you can kind of lose track of them a little bit because they're not having to pay,

they're not having to check in every year. And so that might have contributed to what happened a little bit. Who pays which, which department, which vote would that come from? Do you know? I'm not sure. Ministry of Health, again, that might be a question that, um, we've got some more submit. It sounds like you've got good, you've got some good question to answer here, so I'll save us asking them.

Thanks. Yeah. Alright. Well look, sorry members, anyone No further questions. Very helpful, thank you. Um, thank you. This is a, a topic that, um, as, as parliamentarians, we enjoy the opportunity to be learning what we're learning on topics like this. So thank you very much and hopefully we can help.

So thank you. And we now have, um, you're welcome to remain online, of course, family associates for, sorry, fertility Associates who again, are coming in via Zoom. Right. Good afternoon. Good afternoon. Um, and we have, uh, Suzanne Chuan and obviously one other.

Yeah. So, um, yeah, so my name is, uh, Alex Price. I'm the Chief Executive of Fertility Associates. Um, so thank you for the invitation to make a verbal submission. Uh, so just, uh, I'll give a sort of a brief overview, uh, for about five minutes and then, uh, invite questions.

So just by way of background, uh, fertility Associates started in 1987. Uh, we've close to 40 years offering IVF and other fertility treatment to Kiwis. Uh, we operate from six IVF clinics across the country. So Auckland, Hamilton, Taronga, uh, Wellington, Christchurch, and Dunedin.

Uh, and we also have fi uh, five clinics in Malaysia, one clinic in Thailand. Uh, so to give you an idea, around 5,000 patients, uh, will start IVF treatment with us, uh, this year. So here in New Zealand we provide about 75%, uh, of all the IVF treatment in the country.

Uh, and at this point in time, we have about 14,000 patients who have treatment and, uh, have samples and storage with us. So, uh, in terms of my background as the chief executive, so, uh, my original training was in chemical engineering, uh, but I have completed a postgraduate certificate in reproductive medicine.

Uh, and I've been with FIA Fertility Associates for close to 20 years. Uh, I also served on the board of the Fertility Society of Australia, uh, and New Zealand, and that, that represents those of us working in the sector. So I do have some knowledge of the situation in Australia. Um, I just wanted to make a couple of points, um, in terms of the submission, some of the comments, so IVF was sort of originally developed, um, in 1978 to help, uh, with infertility.

So the idea is that we'd perform an IVF cycle, uh, replace an embryo, and ideally that would lead to a live birth. But fertility treatment is actually expanded, uh, since that time. So of course we treat infertility, that's the main work we do. Uh, but we also pro, uh, assist in the treatment of genetic conditions, uh, through pre-implantation genetic diagnosis, uh, social infertility, which is about assisting, uh, providing reproductive options for members of the, uh, rainbow community.

Uh, and of course the main focus we're talking about today, which is fertility preservation. Um, so this is about the freezing of eggs or sperm, uh, for people who are going to have treatment usually, uh, oncology treatment, which will compromise their reproductive potential. So before having oncology treatment, we can freeze their eggs or sperm, uh, and, uh, those patients can return to us later on and, uh, have, uh, fertility treatment.

So, and I think that's actually become an increasing area of work for us. Uh, and there's a couple of reasons, reasons for that. So one is there's much more awareness of, uh, the availability, fertility preservation. Now, uh, and also, and this is actually really good news. The survival rates, uh, for children and teenagers from cancer has actually also improved substantially.

So we have more awareness, uh, and more patients, uh, able to have that option in future. So if you look at that expansion in fertility treatment and the number of patients we can, we treat, uh, you can see that we've had a significant increase, uh, in the volume of stored material since the heart attack was first put into place over 20 years ago.

So, you know, and that's kind of represented as I say, in those 14,000 patients that we have, uh, with that have material stored with us at the moment in New Zealand. So, you know, we aren't opposed to revisions of a heart act, but our submission was very much saying that we have to be quite careful, uh, in this space.

We do actually believe that clear legislation with timeframes is actually really important. Um, and that's basically around the interest of the potential child patients themselves and actually something that's workable for clinics. Uh, and we don't actually think that an open-ended flexible period is workable.

And I think the concept is something that, uh, clearly many people, uh, do struggle with. You know, how long is too long? You know, one of the things we do consider with a potential child is I think most people would like to think that, uh, a potential child will know their own parents so that the, a child will not lose a parent before they reach adulthood.

So the question you then have to ask yourselves is how old is too old for fertility treatment and how old is too long for material to be stored for? And pretty much the consensus in the sector at the moment in many of these countries is this idea of a check in point, uh, at least once every 10 years.

And in some countries it's five years, just so that people really think about, you know, what am I going to do with my material, with my stored samples? So we, we do believe that these fixed time periods are quite important. Um, I think one of the other things to bear in mind is that eCard itself rarely actually, uh, declines applications.

So as long as we make an application before the 10 years, eCard actually normally answer. And I would, I cannot actually think of a case where they have declined, uh, an oncology related application. So, uh, you know, our view is that, uh, the 10 year limit, uh, is important.

Uh, but it is, uh, you know, an expanding issue with more, uh, frozen material and storage. And, uh, I think it's great. The petition has actually drawn attention to the issue. Um, but specifically if the Ministry of Health, which is where the funding of, uh, storage and material can assist with storage funding beyond that 10 year period, uh, we should be able to, you know, find a solution to this problem within the legislation.

Thank you. Um, thank you. Um, members questions? Um, I, I have a question. So you, you've got 75% of the, of, of the market, as I say, so you are, so there's other organizations that are storing in, in the, doing the same thing as yourselves?

Yeah. So there's two, two other clinics in Auckland Fertility Plus, which is the ADA owned or, uh, owned unit in Green Lane and Reprimand, which is another private clinic. Um, and so with, uh, you are under obligation by law to do the notification that the 10 year, um, and the case that's given rise to today, given there was a letter sent, um, and you had fulfilled your legal obligations by sending the letter.

So, so our obligation, well, the obligation actually sits with the patient to make the application. Uh, and one of the changes that's been worked through at the moment to actually make this process much easier is for clinics to apply on behalf. But I think you, you also have to remember that, uh, patients have ownership or guardianship or, you know, primary sort of control over what they want to do with their reproductive material.

It's a difficult situation if you start saying that clinics are allowed to make application and store material for longer. If a patient may not, may not actually be aware of that. If a patient dies, for example, how would, how would, you know?

Uh, well, we do, uh, monitor, um, you know, death notices and things of that nature, uh, and we are often contacted by people if they know that they, uh, you know, some relation of theirs has had fertility treatment and, and has material stored with us. Right. So I'm just So under re by regulation, what are you obliged to do?

Uh, well, if, if we get to the 10 years, if we get to the 10 years and there is no, uh, basically approval by ECAT to extend beyond the 10 years by right. We have to throw the material out and there's basically a six month grace period to get that discard done.

Uh, but we can't use it within that six month period. We just have to go through the process of discarding in the laboratory. If the regulations would change that you, you had to make reasonable attempts to locate the individual. And you're aware of this case where the notification, first notification wasn't received because the individual was living in a different address, and the second, by the time the notification was received, it was too late.

What, is there putting some onus or regulatory onus on the Storer? Any comment on that? Uh, I think, you know, uh, I mean unfortunately there is a practical reality here. Um, so, uh, in, uh, you know, 2014, uh, art Act was amended, um, to make very clear what the 10 year limit was, uh, which of course fell in 2024.

And, you know, we try and contact people by the last avail available email, and the point was made by the previous submitter about patients getting, uh, you know, a bill every year, which allows us to keep in contact with them. Yeah. But you know, we have email addresses, we have, we send out registered mail as our final sort of step.

Uh, but I think, you know, we, you know, we're not the CIA, we can't sort of hunt the whole country for people. Um, there are, I think at the moment, a million New Zealanders living outside New Zealand. Um, I think, you know, we go to best efforts, but I'm not sure we can be asked to do any more than that.

Well, what if they stop paying the bill? What happens then? Yeah, five years in arrears. Yeah. So if a patient stops paying the bill, then as part of the original consent, we do have the ability to discard the material. Okay. Uh, and you know, many patients actually don't want to make a conscious decision to discard because there's so much, uh, not just financial, but emotional investment in creating those embryos.

So they basically just stop paying the bill. Uh, and when we contact them, they often say, well, I thought you just discarded those embryos. So this really is mostly gonna be an issue for the case we've got before us today, where the government, which department is paying the bill for where it's, um, situations like the one we're dealing with today?

Yeah. So what happens is we have a contract, uh, with Toda Aura. There are four regional contracts across the country. So Auckland, Waikato, Wellington, and South Island. Uh, and to Aura in each region, we have a contract for fertility treatment and that includes the storage, uh, of publicly funded patients and, and includes these onco, uh, oncology patients as well.

Alright. Hi Caleb. Very useful. And, uh, members, any other, no other clarification. Thank you. Well, thank you very much, um, Alex, that was, uh, fascinating, uh, to, to understand that. So, um, thank you for your time. Okay. Thank you very much for that. Thank you. Now we have the ministry here,

ministry of Health or Ministry of Health, and we have Ministry of Justice. Would it be useful to actually perhaps you both come to the table together? Um, I think so. 'cause our questions gonna be the same. Yeah, we the same. We can, Mr. Chairman can use the chair.
We got another, another chair there else just, just to give it some shape. So there's no, I, I know how well departments,

so perhaps we start off with, um, Ms. Patton, is that yourself? Myself? Uh, Kira, thanks for the opportunity to come and talk to you. So my name is Suski Pat, I'm the manager of the ethics team at the Ministry of Health. Is That e that's ecat, is it? No, I look after the ecat. You're not e implement committee? I, my team looks after the secretariat of ecat. Right, okay.

So we kind of manage the operational side of, uh, legislation. I was wondering about how that Yeah. Uh, and with me, with me, I have Kate, Rick B and Ben Clayton, and they are part of the regulatory policy team at the Ministry of Health and my Justice colleagues. Good. I'm Jenny Marchman from the Policy Group at Justice.

Alright, well look, and thanks for joining. So, so we'll get you to start anyway. Sure. Anyway, um, Ms. Patton and then we'll Great, thank you. I mean, I think firstly I would like to acknowledge Ms. Chapman as well and your son and just to acknowledge the difficulties you've had and your negative experience and just to say we acknowledge your concerns with Sister.

Um, and so I I'll just quickly run through Yeah. As of has done a summary of our submissions. Very good paper too, I might say. Yeah. Great. Um, so, um, firstly, as you know, the petition relates to the Human Assisted Reproductive Technology Act. The Heart Act as we call it, um, which sets up the ethics committees that sit in my team.

The ACT is administered by justice, and so Justice is responsible for the drafting and the changing of the act, but the operation largely sits with the Ministry of Health in terms of supporting the ethics teams. Um, as we've already discussed, section 10 sets the maximum storage period of 10 years. Uh, the 10 years can be extended by application to the ethics committee.

So my team manages that application process. Um, there's no limit to the extension length that Arch can give on those applications, and there's no limit to the number of times.
